comments.text: The result should be true for every $\alpha \in \mathbb R^+ \setminus \mathbb N$. For $\alpha = 1/2$, it is e.g. equivalent with the existence of infinitely many Sophie Germain primes, for which one guess there are $2C(1+o(1)) \frac{x}{\ln^2 x}$ many up to $x$. Here $C$ is Hardy–Littlewood's twin prime constant (somewhat confirming the connection). In general, one indeed expects for fixed valid $\alpha$ ($\alpha \in \mathbb N$ are excluded for obvious reasons, where $\alpha=1$ is trivially a special case) the number of valid primes indeed to be of the order $\frac{x}{\ln^2 x}$.
This looks comparable in difficulty to the twin prime conjecture.
